refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation allows design parameters (e.g., hydraulic response time, travel speed envelope, load handling efficiency) to be evaluated independently of operational context.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under controlled duty cycle conditions (load mass, travel distance, terrain type, dump height per customer specification), the NGHE solution completes the load→travel→dump cycle in time ≤ CT_target, measured across a representative sample (n ≥ 30 cycles) with p95 ≤ CT_target. Verify that no design bottleneck (e.g., hydraulic lag, mechanical constraint, control latency) prevents the operator from executing the cycle within the target window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to load material within the defined load time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to travel from load site to dump site within the defined travel time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator to dump material within the defined dump time envelope.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize idle/transition time between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into sequential sub-operations (load, travel, dump) with independent timing budgets that sum to CT_target, each optimized for its operational constraints.
DP2: Implement a centralized cycle controller that orchestrates all sub-operations via state machine logic, with feedback loops to detect and compensate for delays in real-time.

DP3: Design the powertrain and actuation system to deliver peak performance (acceleration, speed, force) sufficient to complete each sub-operation within its allocated time budget under worst-case site conditions.

DP4: Establish a predictive load-balancing mechanism that pre-adjusts operational parameters (e.g., travel speed, load mass distribution) based on site conditions and historical cycle data to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target.
DP5: Implement redundant or parallel execution paths for critical sub-operations (e.g., dual hydraulic circuits for load/dump) to eliminate single-point delays.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP2 + DP3 (integrated): Decompose the cycle into independent timing budgets (DP1), orchestrate via state machine control with feedback (DP2), and size powertrain/actuation to meet peak demands (DP3). This combination is solution-neutral, supports modularity, and avoids over-specification of redundancy or prediction until risk analysis justifies it.

potential_sources: DP1 ↔ DP3: Timing budgets (DP1) must be validated against powertrain capability (DP3); if budgets are unrealistic, iteration is required. Recommend sensitivity analysis on CT_target allocation.
DP2 ↔ DP1: State machine logic (DP2) depends on accurate sub-operation timing models (DP1); poor characterization of load/travel/dump durations will degrade controller effectiveness.
DP2 ↔ DP3: Real-time feedback loops (DP2) require instrumentation and actuation response times compatible with powertrain design (DP3); latency coupling must be quantified.

Site conditions (external): All three DPs are sensitive to site variability (terrain, load mass, ambient conditions); this is a source of coupling to the operational environment, not internal to the design.
coupling_check.notes: The selected combination exhibits acceptable loose coupling if timing budgets are validated early and feedback loop latencies are characterized. DP4 (predictive load-balancing) introduces additional complexity and data dependency; defer unless p95 target cannot be met with DP1+DP2+DP3. DP5 (redundancy) is not justified at this stage without failure mode analysis.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: CT_target is achievable given site conditions and load mass constraints. RISK: If CT_target is set without engineering validation, timing budgets (DP1) will be infeasible. MITIGATION: Conduct early feasibility study with site data.

ASSUMPTION: Feedback sensors (e.g., position, pressure, speed) are available and reliable. RISK: Sensor failures or latency will degrade DP2 effectiveness. MITIGATION: Specify sensor redundancy and validation logic in DP2 design.

ASSUMPTION: Powertrain can be sized to meet peak demands without excessive cost or weight. RISK: Over-sizing may violate other constraints (e.g., fuel consumption, structural limits). MITIGATION: Trade-off analysis required during DP3 realization.

ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, load variability) are bounded and characterized. RISK: Extreme outliers (e.g., unusually heavy loads, soft ground) may violate p95 target. MITIGATION: Define duty cycle limits and operational policies (per constraints); monitor in-field performance.

ASSUMPTION: State machine logic (DP2) can be implemented with acceptable latency and reliability. RISK: Complex state transitions or software bugs may introduce unpredictable delays. MITIGATION: Formal verification or extensive testing of control logic.

ASSUMPTION: Load, travel, and dump sub-operations are truly independent and can be optimized in isolation. RISK: Hidden dependencies (e.g., load position affects travel stability) may couple sub-operations. MITIGATION: Validate independence assumptions during detailed design.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) the acceptable range of CT_target and how it was derived (e.g., customer productivity targets, site constraints); (2) the definition of 'site conditions' and duty cycle (load mass range, terrain types, ambient conditions); (3) the measurement methodology for p95 (e.g., number of cycles, statistical confidence); (4) any constraints on powertrain size, cost, or emissions that may conflict with cycle time optimization. Additionally, introduce a secondary FR to address robustness: 'The NGHE solution shall maintain p95 ≤ CT_target across the defined duty cycle and site conditions without operator intervention.' This clarifies the role of feedback control (DP2) and predictive mechanisms (DP4) in handling variability.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Integrated cycle timing, state machine orchestration, and powertrain/actuation sizing for autonomous ground vehicle operation

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission/task directives from NGHE Solution; Vehicle interprets into cycle timing budgets and state transitions

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports cycle state, timing metrics, powertrain load, and actuation feedback to NGHE Solution and Teleoperation for monitoring

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints/alerts from Safety Services; Vehicle adjusts timing budgets and state machine transitions; exports safety-critical state to Safety Services

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with Teleoperation for remote command override and with NGHE Solution for coordination updates

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: DP1 (timing budgets), DP2 (state machine), and DP3 (powertrain sizing) are all core Vehicle responsibilities. Timing orchestration, control logic, and actuation are Vehicle-native. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert constraints may originate externally, but Vehicle owns the response logic.

Poor containment: NGHE is a mission/task planner, not a real-time cycle executor. DP2 (state machine control) and DP3 (powertrain sizing) are Vehicle-level concerns. DP1 timing budgets would diffuse across Vehicle and Safety Services. High coupling risk.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a remote interface/override layer, not an autonomous control system. DP2 and DP3 require Vehicle autonomy; DP1 timing is Vehicle-internal. Significant diffusion if state machine logic placed here.

Poor containment: Safety Services monitors and constrains, but does not own cycle timing, state orchestration, or powertrain sizing. DP1, DP2, DP3 are operational Vehicle concerns; Safety Services provides guardrails via SafetyAlert interface.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated DP (timing budgets + state machine + powertrain sizing) is fundamentally a Vehicle-level control and resource allocation problem. The Vehicle module owns the real-time execution loop, actuation hardware, and feedback sensors. Timing budgets decompose the Vehicle's operational cycle; state machine orchestrates Vehicle state transitions; powertrain sizing ensures Vehicle can meet peak demands. External modules (NGHE, Safety Services, Teleoperation) interact via well-defined interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry, Network Link) to constrain or direct Vehicle behavior, but do not own the internal control logic.
containment_risks: Risk: If timing budget allocation is driven by mission-level task complexity (NGHE domain), there may be pressure to push DP1 logic into NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Establish clear boundary—NGHE specifies task deadlines; Vehicle owns internal timing decomposition.

Risk: Safety constraints (SafetyAlert) may require dynamic adjustment of state machine transitions or powertrain limits. Mitigation: Vehicle state machine must be designed to accept SafetyAlert inputs and adjust DP2/DP3 parameters without external re-architecture.

Risk: Teleoperation override may conflict with autonomous state machine logic. Mitigation: Define override protocol in Vehicle module; Teleoperation sends commands via Network Link; Vehicle arbitrates between autonomous and remote control.

Risk: Powertrain sizing (DP3) may depend on mission profile (NGHE domain). Mitigation: Vehicle owns sizing logic; NGHE provides mission constraints via Tasking; Vehicle computes required powertrain capacity.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice required. The integrated DP (DP1 + DP2 + DP3) is well-suited to Vehicle containment. However, clarify the interface contract: (1) Tasking carries mission deadlines and task sequence; Vehicle translates to timing budgets. (2) SafetyAlert carries operational constraints; Vehicle adjusts state machine and powertrain limits. (3) Telemetry exports cycle state and resource utilization for monitoring. (4) Network Link enables Teleoperation override with explicit arbitration logic in Vehicle. This keeps DP logic cohesive in Vehicle while maintaining modularity across system.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Cycle time measurement (p95 ≤ CT_target) is directly observable via Vehicle telemetry exports (Telemetry interface). State machine transitions and timing budget adherence are instrumentable within Vehicle module. Powertrain load and actuation feedback enable post-hoc analysis of cycle decomposition fidelity. Acceptance criteria are measurable under agreed duty cycle and site conditions via Vehicle-native instrumentation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR allocation to Vehicle IM is clear and unambiguous. Vehicle owns the integrated cycle execution (load→travel→dump) via DP1 (timing budgets), DP2 (state machine orchestration), and DP3 (powertrain sizing). External modules (NGHE, Safety Services, Teleoperation) constrain or direct Vehicle behavior via well-defined interfaces but do not own cycle execution logic. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are enforced via SafetyAlert interface and Vehicle state machine response logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The integrated DP (DP1 + DP2 + DP3) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. DP1 decomposes the cycle into independent timing budgets, enabling predictable scheduling and resource allocation. DP2 orchestrates state transitions with feedback, ensuring robust cycle progression under operational variability. DP3 sizes powertrain/actuation to meet peak demands, guaranteeing physical capability. The combination is solution-neutral (avoids over-specification of redundancy or prediction), supports modularity (each DP is independently testable), and is verifiable against CT_target via cycle time measurement. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Timing budget allocation (DP1) may diffuse if mission-level task complexity (NGHE domain) drives deadline pressure without clear Vehicle-level decomposition boundary. Mitigation: Establish contract—NGHE specifies task deadlines and sequence via Tasking interface; Vehicle owns internal timing budget allocation and state sequencing. Document DP1 decomposition logic in Vehicle module specification.

Risk: State machine control logic (DP2) may diffuse if SafetyAlert constraints require frequent re-architecture of transitions. Mitigation: Design Vehicle state machine to accept SafetyAlert inputs as runtime parameters (e.g., speed limits, cycle abort triggers) rather than structural changes. Encapsulate constraint response logic within Vehicle module.

Risk: Powertrain sizing (DP3) may diffuse if mission profile variability (NGHE domain) is not clearly separated from Vehicle-level capacity planning. Mitigation: Vehicle computes required powertrain capacity based on worst-case mission constraints provided by NGHE via Tasking. Sizing logic remains Vehicle-owned; NGHE provides input parameters only.

Risk: Teleoperation override (Network Link interface) may conflict with autonomous state machine logic if arbitration is not explicitly owned. Mitigation: Vehicle module must own override arbitration logic; define priority rules (e.g., safety-critical state machine transitions cannot be overridden; non-critical commands queued). Document in Vehicle control specification.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment of integrated DP within Vehicle module (containment_score: 0.92, diffusion_score: 0.08). DP1 (timing budgets), DP2 (state machine), and DP3 (powertrain sizing) are all core Vehicle responsibilities aligned with real-time cycle execution, actuation hardware, and feedback sensors. External modules interact via four well-defined interfaces: Tasking (mission directives), SafetyAlert (operational constraints), Telemetry (state/metrics export), Network Link (remote override). No structural diffusion detected. Minor diffusion risk arises only if interface contracts are ambiguous or if external modules attempt to own internal Vehicle control logic. Recommend explicit interface specifications and ownership documentation to maintain containment.

recommended_revisions: Formalize interface contract for Tasking: NGHE specifies mission deadlines, task sequence, and load/travel/dump parameters; Vehicle translates to DP1 timing budgets. Document translation logic in Vehicle module specification.

Formalize interface contract for SafetyAlert: Safety Services specifies operational constraints (speed limits, abort conditions, resource thresholds); Vehicle state machine (DP2) accepts constraints as runtime parameters and adjusts transitions accordingly. Encapsulate constraint response logic in Vehicle module.

Formalize interface contract for Telemetry: Vehicle exports cycle state (current phase, elapsed time, powertrain load, actuation feedback) at defined frequency. Enable post-hoc verification of DP1 timing budget adherence and DP3 powertrain utilization against CT_target acceptance criteria.

Formalize interface contract for Network Link: Define Teleoperation override protocol with explicit arbitration rules owned by Vehicle module. Specify which state machine transitions are overridable and which are safety-critical (non-overridable). Document priority logic.

Add Vehicle module specification section: 'DP1 Timing Budget Decomposition' detailing how mission deadlines (from Tasking) are decomposed into load, travel, and dump phase budgets, including contingency allocation and feedback adjustment logic.

Add Vehicle module specification section: 'DP2 State Machine Design' documenting state transitions, SafetyAlert constraint integration, and Teleoperation override arbitration. Include state diagram and transition guard conditions.

Add Vehicle module specification section: 'DP3 Powertrain Sizing Rationale' documenting peak demand calculation, duty cycle analysis, and margin allocation. Link to mission constraints provided by NGHE via Tasking interface.

Establish traceability matrix: FR → DP1/DP2/DP3 → Vehicle module components (timing controller, state machine engine, powertrain controller). Ensure no DP logic is orphaned or duplicated across modules.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM alignment at current level (all three gates pass with high containment score 0.92). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that the integrated DP (DP1 + DP2 + DP3) is sufficiently complex and safety-critical to warrant explicit sub-level decomposition. Specifically: (1) DP1 (timing budget allocation) requires formalization of mission-to-cycle translation logic, which is non-trivial and benefits from sub-FR articulation; (2) DP2 (state machine orchestration) involves constraint integration and override arbitration that must be decomposed to ensure safety-critical transitions are properly guarded; (3) DP3 (powertrain sizing) depends on mission profile inputs that must be explicitly traced. Decomposing to sub-FRs will clarify ownership boundaries, formalize interface contracts, and reduce diffusion risk in a safety-critical domain. The current level is sound but not yet detailed enough for implementation specification.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Translate mission task deadlines and load/travel/dump parameters (from NGHE Tasking interface) into Vehicle-level timing budgets for each cycle phase, with contingency allocation and feedback adjustment logic.

FR1.2: Orchestrate cycle state transitions (load → travel → dump → idle) with real-time feedback control, SafetyAlert constraint integration, and Teleoperation override arbitration, ensuring safety-critical transitions are non-overridable.

FR1.3: Size and allocate powertrain/actuation capacity to meet peak demands across all cycle phases under worst-case mission constraints, with duty cycle margin and performance headroom documentation.

revise.why: Recommended_revisions indicate that DP1, DP2, and DP3 require explicit interface contracts and ownership documentation to prevent diffusion. Sub-level decomposition will formalize these contracts as sub-DPs tied to sub-FRs. Additionally, the current DP description lacks detail on constraint integration (SafetyAlert) and override arbitration (Teleoperation), which are critical for safety-critical systems. Revising DP2 to explicitly include constraint response and arbitration logic will improve clarity and traceability.

assumptions: NGHE provides mission deadlines and task sequence via Tasking interface; Vehicle owns internal timing budget decomposition.
Safety Services specifies operational constraints (speed limits, abort conditions) via SafetyAlert; Vehicle state machine owns constraint response logic.
Teleoperation override is arbitrated by Vehicle module; safety-critical state transitions are non-overridable by design.
Powertrain sizing is based on worst-case mission profile provided by NGHE; Vehicle owns capacity planning and margin allocation.
Cycle time measurement (p95 ≤ CT_target) is directly observable via Vehicle telemetry exports; no external instrumentation required.
Vehicle module has access to real-time feedback (powertrain load, actuation state, cycle phase timing) for state machine control and post-hoc verification.
open_risks: Timing budget allocation (DP1) may diffuse if NGHE mission complexity drives deadline pressure without clear Vehicle-level decomposition boundary. Mitigation: Formalize Tasking interface contract and document DP1 decomposition logic in Vehicle specification.

State machine control logic (DP2) may diffuse if SafetyAlert constraints require frequent re-architecture of transitions. Mitigation: Design state machine to accept SafetyAlert inputs as runtime parameters; encapsulate constraint response logic within Vehicle module.

Powertrain sizing (DP3) may diffuse if mission profile variability is not clearly separated from Vehicle-level capacity planning. Mitigation: Vehicle computes required capacity based on worst-case constraints from NGHE; sizing logic remains Vehicle-owned.

Teleoperation override arbitration may conflict with autonomous state machine logic if priority rules are not explicitly defined. Mitigation: Vehicle module owns override arbitration; document priority rules and non-overridable transition list.

Safety-critical state transitions may be compromised if override arbitration logic is not rigorously tested and validated. Mitigation: Establish acceptance criteria for override arbitration (e.g., safety-critical transitions must reject override within 100 ms); include in Vehicle module verification plan.
evidence_needed: Formalized Tasking interface contract: NGHE specifies mission deadlines, task sequence, load/travel/dump parameters; Vehicle translates to timing budgets. Include translation algorithm and contingency allocation logic.

Formalized SafetyAlert interface contract: Safety Services specifies operational constraints; Vehicle state machine accepts constraints as runtime parameters. Include state diagram with constraint guard conditions.

Formalized Telemetry interface contract: Vehicle exports cycle state (phase, elapsed time, powertrain load, actuation feedback) at defined frequency. Enable post-hoc verification of DP1 timing budget adherence and DP3 powertrain utilization.

Formalized Network Link interface contract: Define Teleoperation override protocol with explicit arbitration rules. Specify which state machine transitions are overridable and which are safety-critical (non-overridable).
Vehicle module specification sections: (1) DP1 Timing Budget Decomposition, (2) DP2 State Machine Design with constraint integration and override arbitration, (3) DP3 Powertrain Sizing Rationale.
Traceability matrix: FR → FR1.1/FR1.2/FR1.3 → DP1/DP2/DP3 → Vehicle module components (timing controller, state machine engine, powertrain controller, override arbitrator).

Safety analysis: FMEA or FTA for state machine transitions, override arbitration, and timing budget adherence under fault conditions (e.g., SafetyAlert constraint violation, Teleoperation override during safety-critical transition).

Acceptance test plan: Cycle time measurement (p95 ≤ CT_target) under nominal and off-nominal conditions (e.g., SafetyAlert speed limit, Teleoperation override, powertrain load variation). Include duty cycle and site conditions.
